agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. B2B customers explicitly depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's compliance posture, contractual obligations, and the data lifecycle of legally significant records.","bounded_context":"Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management — intersects with Compliance, Billing, Dispute Resolution, and Partner Integration bounded contexts","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The immutable record of a completed delivery including proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient confirmation, chain of custody, and associated metadata. A legally significant document in logistics."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (PoD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient at the correct location. Required for invoice disputes, insurance claims, and regulatory audits. Typical legal retention: 6+ years."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long specific categories of data are stored, driven by legal requirements, contractual obligations, and business needs — not by storage cost alone."},{"term":"Compliance Reporting","definition":"The process by which B2B customers use delivery data to satisfy their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations. Destroying upstream data destroys their ability to comply."},{"term":"Chain of Custody","definition":"The documented trail of who handled a shipment at each stage. Critical for dispute resolution, insurance, and regulatory investigations."},{"term":"Limitation Period","definition":"The legal window during which claims can be brought. In England & Wales: 6 years for breach of contract. Deleting records within this window destroys the company's ability to defend itself."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records that serve as proof of delivery must be retained for the duration of the applicable legal limitation period (6 years in England & Wales, varies by EU member state but typically 3-10 years)","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal constraint, not a policy preference. Destroying records within the limitation period exposes the company to inability to defend contract disputes, insurance claims, and regulatory investigations. No VP can override statute.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record SHALL be deleted while it is within the applicable jurisdiction's limitation period for contract or tort claims"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Tax-relevant delivery records (commercial shipments, cross-border deliveries, VAT-applicable transactions) must be retained per HMRC and EU member state tax authority requirements — typically 6-7 years","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"UK HMRC requires business records for 6 years. EU VAT Directive requires records for periods set by member states (typically 7-10 years). Violation is a criminal/regulatory offense, not a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record associated with a VAT-applicable or customs-relevant transaction SHALL be deleted before the applicable tax authority retention period expires"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Delivery records that B2B customers depend on for their own compliance reporting must be retained for at least the duration specified in the data processing agreement or service contract with that customer","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deleting data that customers contractually depend on for their compliance is a breach of contract and could cause cascading compliance failures for the customer. This is a contractual and legal constraint.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record SHALL be deleted if an active B2B customer contract specifies a retention period that has not yet elapsed"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"GDPR and UK GDPR require that personal data not be retained longer than necessary for its purpose, but 'necessary' includes legal obligations, contract performance, and legitimate interests including defense of legal claims","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GDPR Article 17(3) explicitly exempts data needed for legal claims, legal obligations, and public interest. Retention for compliance IS the GDPR-compliant position. Premature deletion is not a GDPR requirement — it is a misreading of GDPR.","testable_assertion":"Data retention periods SHALL be justified by documented legal bases, and neither over-retention nor premature deletion shall occur without documented legal basis"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Delivery records involved in open disputes, claims, investigations, or litigation holds must never be auto-deleted regardless of any retention policy","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deleting records subject to litigation hold or active dispute is spoliation of evidence — a serious legal violation with sanctions.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record with an active dispute, claim, investigation, or litigation hold flag SHALL be eligible for automated deletion"}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"B2B customer requests delivery data at day 91 for their annual tax audit","expected_behavior":"Under the proposed policy, the data would be destroyed. The customer cannot complete their audit, breaching our contractual obligation and potentially causing them regulatory penalties."},{"scenario":"Consumer files a contract dispute for a delivery that occurred 18 months ago","expected_behavior":"Under the proposed policy, the proof of delivery is destroyed. The company cannot defend itself and will likely lose the dispute by default."},{"scenario":"HMRC or EU tax authority audits cross-border deliveries from 3 years ago","expected_behavior":"Under the proposed policy, records are destroyed. The company faces penalties for failure to produce required records."},{"scenario":"Insurance claim for high-value lost/damaged shipment filed 4 months after delivery","expected_behavior":"Under the proposed policy, chain of custody records are destroyed. The company or its customer cannot substantiate the insurance claim."},{"scenario":"GDPR Subject Access Request for a delivery from 60 days ago under the proposed policy","expected_behavior":"Data still exists and must be provided. But the 90-day window creates an artificial and legally unjustified cliff where SAR responses depend on timing rather than lawful basis."},{"scenario":"Cross-border delivery with customs declarations — records deleted at 90 days","expected_behavior":"Customs authorities in both origin and destination countries may require records for 3-10 years. Deletion at 90 days violates customs regulations in virtually every EU jurisdiction."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to: (1) regulatory fines from HMRC/EU tax authorities for missing records, (2) inability to defend contract disputes leading to default judgments, (3) breach of B2B contracts triggering liability and customer churn, (4) potential GDPR enforcement action if deletion policy lacks proper legal basis analysis, (5) insurance claim denials due to missing chain of custody records. A single lost tax audit or contract dispute could exceed years of storage savings.","operational":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. Operations teams lose ability to investigate delivery failures, identify patterns, and resolve disputes. B2B customers lose access to compliance data, generating support tickets and escalations. Legal team loses ability to defend claims. The 90-day window is shorter than most business reporting cycles (quarterly, annual).","user":"SEVERE NEGATIVE for B2B customers who explicitly depend on this data for compliance reporting — this could be a contract-breaking change causing immediate churn. B2C consumers lose ability to reference past deliveries for disputes. Trust in the platform as a reliable record-keeper is destroyed.","risk":"CRITICAL. This change creates legal, regulatory, contractual, and reputational risk simultaneously. The framing as 'infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes' is itself a risk indicator — it suggests the legal and compliance implications have not been reviewed. Implementing this without legal sign-off would be negligent.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"CRITICAL — lose access to delivery data they depend on for their own regulatory compliance. May trigger contract breach claims and immediate churn."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"HIGH — lose ability to dispute deliveries or reference past orders beyond 90 days. Reduced trust in platform."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team","impact":"CRITICAL — unable to produce records for tax audits. Personal liability risk for finance officers."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team","impact":"CRITICAL — unable to defend contract disputes, spoliation risk for active matters, regulatory exposure across multiple jurisdictions."},{"group":"Operations / Customer Support","impact":"HIGH — lose historical data needed for pattern analysis, dispute resolution, and root cause investigation."},{"group":"Insurance Partners","impact":"HIGH — claims cannot be substantiated without chain of custody records."},{"group":"Regulatory Bodies (HMRC, EU Tax Authorities, Customs)","impact":"DIRECT — the company would be in violation of record-keeping requirements."}]},"confidence":{"level":25,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the proposal as stated is almost certainly illegal in the jurisdictions where the platform operates. The spec frames this as 'no business logic changes' which is factually incorrect — data retention IS business logic in a regulated logistics platform. The proposal lacks any evidence of legal review, regulatory impact assessment, or contractual analysis. Without confirmation that legal counsel has approved 90 days as compliant with all applicable retention obligations, this cannot be accepted.","high_confidence_areas":["UK HMRC requires 6-year record retention for business records","EU limitation periods for contract claims are typically 3-10 years","B2B customers using data for compliance reporting creates contractual retention obligations","Proof of delivery is a legally significant document in logistics","Premature deletion is NOT a GDPR requirement — GDPR explicitly permits retention for legal obligations"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact retention requirements for each specific EU member state the platform operates in","Whether existing B2B contracts specify explicit retention periods or rely on implicit expectations","Whether the platform has separate archival systems that might partially mitigate the risk","Whether 'delivery records' in this proposal includes or excludes proof of delivery and tax-relevant documents"],"assumptions":["The platform does not have a separate long-term archive that would retain legally required records independently of the proposed deletion","Delivery records include proof of delivery, timestamps, and chain of custody information","B2B customer contracts include expectations or explicit terms around data availability","No legal review has been conducted on this proposal given the framing as 'infrastructure optimization'"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but data retention for delivery records in a regulated logistics platform IS business logic. Retention periods are driven by legal obligations (tax law, limitation periods, customs regulations), contractual obligations (B2B compliance reporting), and operational requirements (dispute resolution). A 90-day retention period contradicts the legal minimum retention requirements in virtually every jurisdiction the platform operates in. This is not an infrastructure decision — it is a legal and compliance decision that requires legal counsel review before any technical work begins.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Finance/Tax Team","Legal/Compliance Team","Regulatory Bodies","B2C Consumers"],"options":["A: Reject the proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention","B: Engage legal counsel to determine minimum retention periods per jurisdiction and data category, then implement tiered retention (e.g., anonymize personal data after legal minimum while retaining business records)","C: Implement data lifecycle tiers — hot storage (90 days), warm archive (2 years), cold archive (7 years) — to reduce storage costs without deleting legally required records"],"recommendation":"Option C. The legitimate business goal (reduce storage costs) can be achieved through storage tiering without deleting records. Move records older than 90 days to cheaper cold storage. This addresses the cost concern without creating legal, contractual, or regulatory exposure. Option B should run in parallel to ensure retention periods are formally documented. Under NO circumstances should records be deleted at 90 days without explicit legal counsel sign-off for each jurisdiction and data category."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"90-day deletion violates UK HMRC 6-year business record retention requirement","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"90-day deletion destroys proof of delivery within the 6-year limitation period for contract claims in England & Wales","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"90-day deletion breaches obligations to B2B customers who depend on delivery data for compliance reporting","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"Proposal lacks documented legal basis analysis required by GDPR/UK GDPR for changes to data retention","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"No carve-out for records subject to active disputes or litigation holds","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"},{"violation":"Framing as 'no business logic changes' is incorrect and indicates insufficient impact analysis","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3, BR-4, BR-5"}]}
